content_system.purpose: Content design rules for UI copy, labels, helper text, metadata, card summaries, and structured page messaging.
principles: Content must reduce confusion and support action.
Use clear, literal wording over clever wording.
Write for scanning first, then detail.
Keep labels and helper text short but specific.
The same concept should use the same wording across the product.

avoid: overly technical jargon when simpler words exist

rules: Buttons should use action-oriented wording.
Labels should describe the field or action plainly.
Helper text should clarify ambiguity, not repeat the label.
Empty, loading, error, and success messages must follow a consistent pattern.
Use one canonical name for each important concept.
Do not alternate between similar labels such as Catalog/Products/Printers unless meaning truly differs.
Keep CTA wording and navigation wording aligned.
Headlines must quickly explain purpose or value.
Avoid vague or overly clever headlines.
Use shorter headlines for utility pages and dashboards.
Use stronger value framing for public landing sections.
Paragraphs should be short and readable.
Break dense information into bullets or grouped sections when helpful.
Avoid long uninterrupted text blocks in UI surfaces.

labels: Must be specific, short, and predictable.
helper_text: Must explain format, consequence, or intent.
status_messages: Must explain what happened and what to do next when relevant.
empty_states: Must explain why nothing is shown and what to do next.

rules: Card titles should be concise.
Card descriptions should be 1 to 3 short lines when possible.
Metadata should remain visually secondary.
Do not overload cards with too many competing messages.
Rank labels such as #1 or #2 should support hierarchy, not dominate it.
Product titles should remain the key content anchor.
Summary text should explain why the item is ranked well in 1 to 2 short lines.
Specification labels must be short and recognizable.
Values should be easy to scan.
Internal actions and outbound actions must use clearly different copy.
Input labels must explain what value is expected.
Units must be shown where relevant.
Result labels must make cost meaning obvious.
Supporting explanation should be short and confidence-building.
Admin labels should prioritize speed and accuracy.
Action wording should be literal and consequence-aware.
Status text should be operational, not promotional.
Use plain language where possible.
Break long legal text into clear sections and subheadings.
Use bullet lists when clarity improves.
Metadata such as date, author, category, or status should be compact and secondary.
Metadata should never visually outrank the main title.
Use lists when scanning is more useful than prose.
List items should be parallel in structure where possible.
State what failed.
Say what the user can try next.
Avoid generic messages unless no more detail is safe or useful.
Confirm the completed action clearly.
Keep routine success messages brief.
Use outcome-focused action verbs.
Keep labels concise.
Do not use vague wording like 'Click Here' or 'Continue' without context.
Use descriptive headings.
Keep important terms visible near the start of labels and headings.
Support scanability with clear section naming.

anti_patterns: Helper text that repeats the label without adding value
Generic empty/error messages with no recovery path
Cards stuffed with too much text
Dashboard language that sounds like marketing copy
implementation_notes_for_ai: Use the same wording for the same concept everywhere.
Prefer clarity and scanability over cleverness.
When rewriting copy, preserve existing user intent and IA structure unless the wording is genuinely confusing.
